critique.summary: The article describes 'Get Shit Done' (GSD), a lightweight meta-prompting, context engineering, and spec-driven development system designed to improve the reliability and quality of AI-assisted coding with Claude Code and other AI runtimes. It outlines the system's workflow, features, commands, and technical details, emphasizing its ability to maintain context quality, automate task execution, and produce clean, verifiable code commits.
mainPoints: GSD is a development system that uses meta-prompting and context engineering to enhance AI code generation quality, specifically addressing context degradation in Claude Code.
It supports multiple AI runtimes including Claude Code, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, Codex, Copilot, and Antigravity.
The system automates phases of software development: project initialization, phase discussion, planning, execution, verification, and milestone management.
GSD uses multi-agent orchestration to parallelize tasks and maintain fresh context windows, improving efficiency and output quality.
Each task results in atomic git commits for traceability and easier debugging.
The system includes commands for various workflow steps, configuration options, and security measures to protect sensitive data.
GSD is designed for solo developers or small teams who want to avoid complex enterprise workflows while maintaining high-quality code generation.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about GSD's functionality and features are consistent with the detailed technical descriptions and command references provided in the article. The repository is publicly available on GitHub with extensive documentation, which supports the existence and described capabilities of the system. The mention of integration with known AI runtimes like Claude Code, Codex, and Copilot aligns with known technologies. However, the article is primarily promotional and technical documentation from the developer's repository, so it lacks independent third-party verification or multiple perspectives.
considerations: The article does not provide independent user reviews or third-party evaluations of GSD's effectiveness or reliability.
It does not compare GSD's performance or usability directly against other spec-driven development tools in a systematic way.
Security considerations mention protecting sensitive files but do not detail potential risks of automated AI code generation or mitigation strategies beyond deny lists.
The system's reliance on AI models like Claude Code assumes their availability and performance, which may vary over time or by user environment.
The article does not discuss potential limitations or failure cases of the system in complex or large-scale projects.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the effectiveness and practicality of the GSD (Get Shit Done) AI development framework compared to alternatives like Superpowers and Claude Code. Users share mixed experiences, highlighting issues such as token consumption, verbosity, planning overhead, and challenges in maintaining or scaling projects, while some praise its productivity gains and customization potential.

comments.author: gtirloni
comments.text: I was using this and superpowers but eventually, Plan mode became enough and I prefer to steer Claude Code myself. These frameworks are great for fire-and-forget tasks, especially when there is some research involved but they burn 10x more tokens, in my experience. I was always hitting the Max plan limits for no discernable benefit in the outcomes I was getting. But this will vary a lot depending on how people prefer to work.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced view based on personal experience, noting token inefficiency but acknowledging utility for certain tasks, making it a credible and constructive insight.

comments.author: DamienB
comments.text:
I've compared this to superpowers and the classic prd->task generator. And I came away convinced that less is more. At least at the moment. gsd performed well, but took hours instead of minutes. 
Having a simple explanation of how to create a PRD followed by a slightly more technical task list performed much better. It wasn't that grd or superpowers couldn't find a solution, it's just that they did it much slower and with a lot more help. 
For me, the lesson was that the workflow has changed, and we that we can't apply old project-dev paradigms to this new/alien technology. 
There's a new instruction manual and it doesn't build on the old one.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a comparative evaluation with emphasis on efficiency and workflow adaptation, providing a thoughtful critique that aligns with practical concerns about tool complexity.

comments.author: yoaviram
comments.text:
I've been using GSD extensively over the past 3 months. I previously used speckit, which I found lacking. GSD consistently gets me 95% of the way there on complex tasks. That's amazing. The last 5% is mostly "manual" testing. We've used GSD to build and launch a SaaS product including an agent-first CMS (whiteboar.it).

It's hard to say why GSD worked so much better for us than other similar frameworks, because the underlying models also improved considerably during the same period. What is clear is that it's a huge productivity boost over vanilla Claude Code.
comments.analysis: A positive firsthand account highlighting significant productivity improvements, lending credibility to GSD's effectiveness, though acknowledging some manual effort remains.

comments.author: DIVx0
comments.text:
I’ve tried GSD several times. I actually like the verbosity and it’s a simple chore for Claude to refresh project docs from GSD planning docs.

Like most spec driven development tools, GSD works well for greenfield or first few rounds of “compound engineering.” However, like all others, the project gets too big and GSD can’t manage to deliver working code reliably.

Agents working GSD plans will start leaving orphans all over, it won’t wire them up properly because verification stages use simple lexical tools to search code for implementation facts. I tried giving GSD some ast aware tools but good luck getting Claude to reliably use them.

Ultimately I put GSD back on the shelf and developed my own “property graph” based planner that is closer to Claude “plan mode” but the design SOT is structured properties and not markdown. My system will generate docs from the graph as user docs. Agents only get tasked as my “graph” closes nodes and re-sorts around invariants, then agents are tasked directly.
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed critique of GSD's limitations in scaling and verification, suggesting the need for more advanced tooling, which is a valuable technical perspective.

comments.author: Andrei_dev
comments.text:
250K lines in a month — okay, but what does review actually look like at that volume?

I've been poking at security issues in AI-generated repos and it's the same thing: more generation means less review. Not just logic — checking what's in your .env, whether API routes have auth middleware, whether debug endpoints made it to prod.

You can move that fast. But "review" means something different now. Humans make human mistakes. AI writes clean-looking code that ships with hardcoded credentials because some template had them and nobody caught it.

All these frameworks are racing to generate faster. Nobody's solving the verification side at that speed.
comments.analysis: Raises an important concern about the risks of rapid AI code generation without thorough human review, highlighting a critical aspect often overlooked.

comments.author: recroad
comments.text:
I use openspec and love it. I’m doing 5-7x with close to 100% of code AI generated, and shipping to production multiple times a day. I work on a large sass app with hundreds of customers. Wrote something here:

https://zarar.dev/spec-driven-development-from-vibe-coding-t...
comments.analysis: Shares a successful use case of a spec-driven framework with high productivity and production deployment, supporting the viability of such approaches.

comments.author: vinnymac
comments.text:
I tried this for a week and gave up. Required far too much back and forth. Ate too many tokens, and required too much human in the loop.

For this reason I don’t think it’s actually a good name. It should be called planning-shit instead. Since that’s seemingly 80%+ of what I did while interacting with this tool. And when it came to getting things done, I didn’t need this at all, and the plans were just alright.
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration with the tool's overhead and token usage, reflecting a common user experience that highlights usability challenges.

comments.author: seneca
comments.text:
I've tried several of these sorts of things, and I keep coming away with the feeling that they are a lot of ceremony and complication for not much value. I appreciate that people are experimenting with how to work with AI and get actual value, but I think pretty much all of these approaches are adding complexity without much, or often any, gain.

That's not a reason to stop trying. This is the iterative process of figuring out what works.
comments.analysis: Offers a measured critique about the complexity of AI development frameworks, encouraging continued experimentation despite current limitations.

comments.author: theodorewiles
comments.text: I think the research / plan / execute idea is good but feels like you would be outsourcing your thinking. Gotta review the plan and spend your own thinking tokens!
comments.analysis: Highlights the importance of human oversight and critical thinking in AI-assisted workflows, a valid caution against over-reliance on automation.

comments.author: smusamashah
comments.text:
There should be an "Examples" section in projects like this one to show what has actually been made using it. I scrolled to the end and was really expecting an example the way it's being advertised.

If it was game engine or new web framework for example there would be demos or example projects linked somewhere.
comments.analysis: Constructive suggestion for improving project documentation and user confidence by providing concrete examples.

comments.author: loveparade
comments.text:
"I am a super productive person that just wants to get shit done"

Looked at profile, hasn't done or published anything interesting other than promoting products to "get stuff done"

This is like the TODO list book gurus writing about productivity
comments.analysis: This comment is dismissive and ad hominem, lacking substantive critique and thus less credible.

comments.author: jatora
comments.text: Another heavily overengineered AND underengineered abomination. I'm convinced anyone who advocates for these types of tools would find just as much success just prompting claude code normally and taking a little bit to plan first. Such a waste of time to bother with these tools that solve a problem that never existed in the first place.
comments.analysis: An overly negative and hyperbolic comment that does not provide constructive feedback or evidence.

comments.author: greenchair
comments.analysis: A short, dismissive comment focused on the name only, offering no useful insight.
